title: Dynamical Josephson Effect Between a Singlet and a Triplet Superconductor
authors: Morten Amundsen, Niladri Banerjee, Igor Žutić
abstract: Phase-sensitive Josephson effect has long been central to identifying unconventional pairing symmetries in superconductors. Although the selection rules governing Josephson junctions (JJs) are generally determined by the symmetries of the constituent superconductors, we demonstrate that this paradigm is modified in the dynamic regime. By modeling a JJ where spin-singlet and spin-triplet superconductors are separated by a two-dimensional electron gas, we show that a time-dependent gate voltage qualitatively changes the underlying selection rules. This modification arises as a consequence of the gate-controlled spin-orbit coupling. A harmonic modulation of the gate voltage generates an oscillatory $\cos φ$ Josephson component which vanishes in the static limit. The resulting charge current contains both dissipationless and dissipative components, with the latter strongly suppressed at low temperatures. This dynamical Josephson effect could transform the use of JJs in qubits, as sources of spin-triplet currents, and as platforms for proximity effects.
summary: This theoretical work analyzes the Josephson effect in a junction between singlet and triplet superconductors under a time-varying gate voltage. It demonstrates that the dynamic modulation induces a novel cos(phi) current component, which is a direct consequence of the spin symmetry mismatch. This effect suggests new ways to use such junctions for spin-triplet current sources in quantum devices.
main_problem: The paper investigates how the selection rules governing the Josephson effect are modified when the junction is driven dynamically, particularly when coupling a spin-singlet and a spin-triplet superconductor.
main_result: A time-dependent gate voltage induces a novel Josephson current component proportional to cos(phi), which is absent in the static limit and arises due to the spin symmetry mismatch between the superconductors.
method: The analysis employs the Keldysh Green function formalism to calculate the time-dependent current, decomposing it into symmetric (dissipationless) and antisymmetric (dissipative) components.
model_or_system: The system is a Josephson junction (JJ) connecting a spin-singlet (SL) and a spin-triplet (SR) superconductor, separated by a 2DEG whose coupling is modulated by a time-dependent gate voltage inducing SOC.
key_observables: The current-phase relation (CPR), the total time-dependent current I(t), and the relative strengths of the dissipationless and dissipative current components.
important_parameters: The driving frequency ($\Omega$), the SOC strength ($\alpha_1$), and the superconducting gap sizes ($\Delta_L, \Delta_R$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a simple SOC model ($\propto \sigma_z k_y$) and uses approximations in the Keldysh formalism, neglecting higher-order frequency mode contributions.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the JJ setup with time-dependent SOC and show the phase accumulation of tunneling particles, while Figure 3 compares the normalized amplitudes of the AC and dissipative currents for singlet-singlet vs. singlet-triplet junctions.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical model (SL/SR JJ with time-dependent SOC) to deriving the current-phase relation using phase accumulation arguments, culminating in the full calculation via the Keldysh formalism to extract the time-dependent current components.
why_interesting: The study of dynamical Josephson effects in unconventional superconductors provides a platform to probe spin-momentum locking and parity selection rules, which are crucial concepts in superconducting spintronics and topological superconductivity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
